title: SEGMENT 2 — The Asn297 N-glycan in detail

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — The Asn297 N-glycan in detail
Okay.
Let's actually look at this sugar tree.
Because everything we say later — about fucose, about galactose, about sialic acid — depends on you having a picture of this molecule in your head.
So let me build it for you, piece by piece.
Picture, please, an asparagine.
It's an amino acid, sitting in the middle of a protein chain.
Asparagines have a little side group sticking off them — a short arm ending in an amide.
Now imagine you take that amide nitrogen and you attach a sugar to it.
That's an N-linked glycosylation.
The "N" stands for nitrogen — the nitrogen of the asparagine side chain.
From that nitrogen, a chain of sugars grows outward, away from the protein, like a tree rooted in the side of the polypeptide.
The first sugar attached to the nitrogen is always the same: N-acetylglucosamine, abbreviated GlcNAc.
Think of it as a glucose with a nitrogen-containing acetyl group hanging off one carbon.
It's the foundation.
Then a second GlcNAc gets stacked on top of the first.
So now we have asparagine — GlcNAc — GlcNAc, a little three-unit stalk reaching out from the protein.
We call this the chitobiose core because it looks like the building block of chitin, the stuff insect shells are made of.
On top of the second GlcNAc, the chain branches.
A mannose sugar gets added — a six-carbon sugar of a particular stereochemistry — and on that mannose, two more mannoses get added, each branching off in a different direction.
So now we have a Y inside the bigger Y of the antibody.
From the asparagine: GlcNAc, GlcNAc, then a branching mannose, then two mannoses splaying off in two directions.
This part of the structure — the three mannoses sitting on the two GlcNAcs — is called the trimannosyl core, and it is the conserved backbone of every IgG N-glycan you'll ever see.
That's the trunk and the first fork.
Now the structure becomes biantennary.
Each of those two outer mannoses can have another GlcNAc added to it — one on each arm — and that gives you what's called the biantennary G0 structure: two GlcNAcs sticking out on two arms.
G0 stands for "galactose zero" — meaning no galactose has been added yet.
This is the most basic mature IgG glycan: chitobiose core, trimannosyl branch, two terminal GlcNAcs.
Two antennas.
From here, the structure can be further decorated.
A galactose can be added onto one of the terminal GlcNAcs, giving you G1 — galactose one — meaning one galactose, on one arm.
Or galactose can be added to both terminal GlcNAcs, giving you G2 — galactose two.

And if the galactose is there, an additional sugar called sialic acid — specifically, N-acetylneuraminic acid, the same molecule that sits on the surface of many of your cells — can cap the galactose, giving you a sialylated structure.
S1 if one galactose is sialylated, S2 if both are.
And then there is the side decoration that matters most for our story today.
Coming off the very first GlcNAc — the one attached to the asparagine, the foundation — there can be a fucose.
Fucose is a six-carbon sugar with a particular structure, and it attaches to the very base of the glycan tree.
We call this the core fucose, and it is added by an enzyme called FUT8 — fucosyltransferase 8 — that does only this one job: stick a fucose on the bottom GlcNAc of an N-glycan.
When the core fucose is present, we call the structure fucosylated.
When it's absent, we call it afucosylated.
And as you'll see in the next several segments, that single sugar — present or absent — controls the most dramatic functional effect in the entire glycoengineering field.
Let me give you the menu of variants.
In a normal human IgG, the Asn297 glycan is, in rough order of abundance: G0F — that is, biantennary, zero galactose, core fucose — about 30% of the population.
G1F — biantennary, one galactose, core fucose — maybe 35%.
G2F — two galactose, core fucose — maybe 15%.

G0 without fucose, G1 without fucose, G2 without fucose, G2FS1 and G2FS2 with various sialic acid combinations, and a handful of more unusual variants — bisecting GlcNAc structures, high-mannose structures, hybrid structures — making up the rest.
In total, roughly thirty distinguishable glycoforms coexist on the same antibody preparation in any given person.
Here's the key point.
In a healthy adult, somewhere between 80 and 95 percent of IgG molecules carry the core fucose.
Only about 5 to 20 percent are naturally afucosylated.
That fraction matters because, as we'll see, those few percent afucosylated molecules are the ones doing the heaviest immune lifting against tumors.
And it's why engineering an antibody to be entirely afucosylated — to drive that fraction from 5% up to 95% or 100% — has such a dramatic effect on potency.
One more anatomical detail before we move on, and it's a beautiful one.
The two Asn297 glycans on the two heavy chains of an IgG are not just floating off into solution.
They sit in a pocket.
The Fc region of IgG folds in such a way that the two heavy-chain CH2 domains face each other across an internal cavity, and the two glycans grow into that cavity, filling it.
They make contact with each other — sugar-to-sugar — and they make contact with the protein surface around them.
The glycan and the protein are wedged together.
The glycan is not just hanging off the outside of the Fc; it is part of the Fc structure.
Remove it entirely and the CH2 domains collapse inward, lose their proper shape, and the Fc stops being recognized by Fc receptors at all.
So when we talk about modifying the glycan, we are not just decorating the antibody — we are reshaping it.
Adding or removing a single sugar at the bottom of the tree subtly changes the geometry of the entire Fc handle.
And that geometry is what determines how the handle docks into Fc receptors on the surface of immune cells.
The glycan is structural.
The glycan is also functional.
The two are inseparable.
This is the molecule.
Now let's talk about how anyone figured any of this out.
